section.title: The Resurrection
section.sectionRationale: On the first day of the week the tomb is empty. An angel rolls back the stone and announces that Jesus has risen; the risen Lord meets the women and sends them to His brothers, while the guards are paid to spread a lie. Death is undone, and the news goes out.
section.thesis: Jesus has risen just as He said: the tomb is empty, the crucified Lord is alive and meets His own, calling them from fear to joy and worship, even as the authorities try to bury the truth under a bribe.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the empty tomb and the angel's announcement; the risen Jesus meeting the women; and the guards bribed to lie about the resurrection.

units.label: The empty tomb
units.note: He has risen, just as He said

verses.text: After the Sabbath, at dawn on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to see the tomb.
Suddenly there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven, rolled away the stone, and sat on it.
His appearance was like lightning, and his clothes were white as snow.
The guards trembled in fear of him and became like dead men.
But the angel said to the women, “Do not be afraid, for I know that you are looking for Jesus, who was crucified.
He is not here; He has {{egeiro|risen}}, just as He said! Come, see the place where He lay.
Then go quickly and tell His disciples, ‘He has risen from the dead and is going ahead of you into Galilee. There you will see Him.’ See, I have told you.”
So they hurried away from the tomb in fear and great {{chara|joy}}, and ran to tell His disciples.

units.label: The risen Lord meets the women

verses.text: Suddenly Jesus met them and said, “Greetings!” They came to Him, grasped His feet, and {{proskyneo|worshiped}} Him.
“Do not be afraid,” said Jesus. “Go and tell My brothers to go to Galilee. There they will see Me.”
While the women were on their way, some of the guards went into the city and reported to the chief priests all that had happened.
And after the chief priests had met with the elders and formed a plan, they gave the soldiers a large sum of money
and instructed them: “You are to say, ‘His disciples came by night and stole Him away while we were asleep.’
If this report reaches the governor, we will satisfy him and keep you out of trouble.”
So the guards took the money and did as they were instructed. And this account has been circulated among the Jews to this very day.

egeiro.gloss: To raise up, to wake, to raise from the dead. The angel's announcement, He has risen, is in the passive, He has been raised: God raised Jesus from the dead, vindicating His Son and defeating death, exactly as Jesus had foretold.

chara.gloss: Joy, gladness. The women leave the tomb with fear and great joy, the fitting response to the resurrection: awe at the holy power of God mingled with the overwhelming gladness that Jesus is alive.

proskyneo.gloss: To bow down, to worship. The women grasp the risen Jesus' feet and worship Him; the resurrection reveals Him as the proper object of worship, the risen Lord who is truly God with us.

basic.context: Early on Sunday, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary come to the tomb. An earthquake and an angel, bright as lightning, roll back the stone as the guards collapse in terror. The angel tells the women not to fear: Jesus is not here, He has risen as He said; they are to tell the disciples He will meet them in Galilee. Running with fear and great joy, they are met by the risen Jesus Himself, whom they worship. Meanwhile the guards report to the priests, who bribe them to spread the story that the disciples stole the body.
basic.hermeneutics: The resurrection is presented as a real event with witnesses, an empty tomb, and physical encounter. Note the two responses to the same empty tomb: the women worship, the leaders concoct a lie. That contrast, and the very existence of the theft story, are part of the evidence. Read the angel's just as He said: the resurrection vindicates all Jesus foretold.
basic.originalLanguages: The angel announces that Jesus has been egeiro, raised, by God. The women leave in fear and great chara, joy. And meeting the risen Lord, they proskyneo, worship Him.
basic.typology: Jesus rising on the third day fulfills the sign of Jonah and His own repeated predictions. The first day of the week, a new beginning, echoes the first day of creation, now the dawn of new creation.
basic.christ: Jesus is the risen Lord, alive after His crucifixion, vindicated by God, worthy of worship, and tenderly calling His failed disciples His brothers. Death could not hold Him; He is the conqueror of the grave.
basic.god: God raised Jesus from the dead, reversing the verdict of the cross and defeating death. He sends His angel to open the tomb and announce the news, and He turns even a hostile cover-up into unwitting testimony that the tomb was empty.
basic.crossReferences: Jonah 1 and Matthew 12 give the third-day sign. Psalm 16 promised God would not let His Holy One see decay. 1 Corinthians 15 declares the resurrection the foundation of the faith. Romans 1 says Jesus was declared Son of God by the resurrection.
basic.oneStory: The stone is rolled away, and the story turns from death to unending life. The crucified Jesus is risen, just as He said, and the same women who watched Him die now worship Him alive. Death, the last enemy since Eden, is broken. The cover-up cannot bury the empty tomb, and the risen Lord gathers His brothers for the mission that will carry this news to the ends of the earth.

context.literary: Matthew's resurrection account answers the sealed and guarded tomb of chapter 27: God's angel undoes what the leaders secured. The women, last at the cross and the burial, are first at the tomb and first to see the risen Lord. The bribing of the guards forms a deliberate counterpart to the angel's true announcement, and the whole sets up the Great Commission that closes the Gospel.
context.historical: Women were not regarded as strong legal witnesses in that culture, so their prominence as first witnesses argues for the account's authenticity rather than invention. The first day of the week became the Christian day of worship. The guards faced death for sleeping on duty, which the priests promise to smooth over. Matthew notes the theft story was still circulating when he wrote, acknowledging the counter-claim openly.
context.canonical: The resurrection fulfills Jesus' own predictions (Matthew 16:21, 17:23, 20:19) and the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:40). It fulfills Psalm 16:10, that God's Holy One would not see decay, cited in Acts 2 and 13. Paul makes it the linchpin of the gospel in 1 Corinthians 15:3-8, 14, 20, the firstfruits of those who have died.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify that Jesus truly rose from the dead as He foretold, vindicated by God and worshiped as Lord, and to expose the cover-up as a lie, contrasting worship of the risen Christ with the hardened rejection of the truth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the historical fact of the resurrection and its first witnesses. It prescribes, implicitly, the response of the women, moving from fear to joy to worship and to going and telling, and it warns against the leaders' willful suppression of the truth.

debates.question: How is the empty tomb best explained?

views.label: The bodily resurrection of Jesus
views.case: The angelic announcement, the physical encounters where the women grasp His feet, the transformation of the fearful disciples, and the rise of the church all point to a real, bodily resurrection; the tomb was empty because Jesus was raised, just as He said.

views.label: The stolen-body theory, and why it fails
views.case: The leaders' own bribe assumes an empty tomb and blames theft; yet the disciples had fled and despaired, the tomb was guarded and sealed, and men who invented a lie would not then die for it, so the theft story collapses under its own weight and actually testifies that the tomb was empty.

debates.bearing: The very existence of the counter-story concedes the empty tomb; weighing the evidence, the resurrection accounts for the facts that the alternatives cannot, and it grounds the church's faith and mission.

study.originalLanguages: The angel's announcement is in the passive: Jesus has been egeiro, raised, for God the Father raised His Son from the dead, vindicating Him and shattering the power of death, exactly as Jesus foretold. The women flee the empty tomb with two emotions held together, fear at the holy power of God and great chara, joy, that their Lord is alive. And when the risen Jesus meets them on the road, they fall and grasp His feet and proskyneo, worship Him, for the resurrection unveils Him as the rightful object of worship, the living Lord who is God with us. Meanwhile the leaders, faced with the same empty tomb, choose a bribe and a lie, and even their cover-up bears witness that the grave could not hold Him.

typology.type: The sign of Jonah, three days
typology.fulfillment: Jesus raised on the third day as He foretold, Matthew 12:40, Jonah 1:17.

typology.type: The first day of the week
typology.fulfillment: The dawn of new creation in the risen Christ, echoing the first day of Genesis, 2 Corinthians 5:17.

typology.type: God's Holy One not abandoned to decay
typology.fulfillment: Psalm 16:10 fulfilled in the resurrection, Acts 2:24-32.

study.christ: Jesus is the risen Lord, alive forevermore after His crucifixion, raised by the Father as the vindication of all He claimed and foretold. Death, which held every human since Adam, could not hold Him; He walked out of the grave and met His own on the road. He is worthy of the worship the women give at His feet, God with us who is now also the conqueror of death. And His first word to failing disciples is grace: He calls them My brothers, restoring the very men who fled, and sends them to Galilee to see Him. The crucified is risen, and everything He said is proven true.
study.god: God the Father is the great actor of Easter: He raised Jesus from the dead, reversing the verdict of the cross and declaring His Son vindicated and victorious. He sends His angel to roll back the stone, not to let Jesus out but to let the witnesses in, and to announce the news the world most needs to hear. He is sovereign even over opposition, so that the leaders' bribe and lie, meant to bury the truth, instead concede the empty tomb and testify against themselves. In raising His Son, God defeats death, secures the hope of all who trust Him, and launches the mission of the gospel to the world.

crossReferences.note: The sign of Jonah, three days and nights, fulfilled in the resurrection.
You will not abandon Your Holy One to see decay, fulfilled in Jesus' rising.
The gospel of Christ died, buried, raised on the third day, and seen by witnesses.
Christ raised as the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, the pledge of our resurrection.

study.oneStory: The stone is rolled away, and the whole story turns from death to unending life. The crucified Jesus is risen, just as He said, and the women who watched Him die now worship Him alive. Death, the last enemy since Eden, is broken, and the way to the tree of life stands open. The leaders' cover-up cannot bury an empty tomb; the truth will out. The risen Lord gathers His brothers for the mission that will carry the news to the ends of the earth, and the Gospel moves to its final scene, the commission on the mountain in Galilee.

quiz.q: What did the angel announce at the empty tomb?

options.text: Jesus is not here; He has risen, just as He said, and will meet His disciples in Galilee.
options.note: Verses 6-7 give the angel's announcement of the resurrection foretold by Jesus.

options.text: That Jesus' body had been moved to another place.
options.note: The tomb was empty because He rose, not because the body was moved; this misses the resurrection.

options.text: That the disciples should mourn at the tomb.
options.note: The angel called them from fear to joyful proclamation, not mourning.

options.text: That Jesus would remain in the grave until the last day.
options.note: The angel declares He has already risen.

quiz.q: How did the women respond when the risen Jesus met them?

options.text: They grasped His feet and worshiped Him, with fear and great joy.
options.note: Verses 8-9 show worship of the risen Lord mixed with awe and joy.

options.text: They were glad to see a respected teacher again.
options.note: It was far more than seeing a teacher; they worshiped Him as Lord, which this understates.

options.text: They ran away in terror and told no one.
options.note: They were afraid but full of joy and ran to tell the disciples.

options.text: They did not recognize Him at all.
options.note: They recognized and worshiped Him at once.

quiz.q: How does the guards' bribe function in Matthew's account?

options.text: The cover-up concedes the tomb was empty and testifies, against the leaders' intent, to the resurrection.
options.note: The theft story assumes an empty tomb, undercutting itself as evidence.

options.text: It proves the disciples really did steal the body.
options.note: Matthew presents this as a paid-for lie, not the truth; the disciples had fled and despaired.

options.text: It shows the guards honestly reported what they saw.
options.note: They reported the truth first, then were bribed to spread a lie.

options.text: It has nothing to do with the resurrection.
options.note: It is directly about explaining away the empty tomb.

quiz.q: What is striking about Jesus calling the disciples His brothers after the resurrection?

options.text: He restores the very men who had fled and denied Him, meeting failure with grace.
options.note: Verse 10 shows the risen Lord's tender restoration of failing disciples.

options.text: He was simply being polite to His followers.
options.note: It is far more than politeness; it is gracious restoration after their desertion, which this misses.

options.text: He no longer regarded them as disciples.
options.note: Calling them brothers draws them closer, not further away.

options.text: He was rebuking them for abandoning Him.
options.note: His word is one of grace and restoration, not rebuke.

questions: The women came expecting a grave and found it empty. Where are you approaching a situation as final and closed that the risen Christ may yet transform?
They left the tomb with fear and great joy at once. How do awe at God's power and joy in the risen Christ live together in your own faith?
The risen Jesus called His deserting disciples His brothers. How does His grace toward those who failed Him encourage you when you have failed Him?
Faced with the same empty tomb, some worshiped and some lied. When the truth about Jesus confronts you, which response is more natural to you, and why?
The resurrection means death is defeated. How would truly believing that Jesus is alive change the way you face your fears this week?

translationNotes.note: The Greek is literally after the Sabbaths, at dawn toward the first of the Sabbaths; it marks the early morning of the first day of the week, Sunday, which became the Christian day of worship.
He has risen translates a passive form, He has been raised, pointing to the Father as the one who raised Jesus; just as He said ties the event to Jesus' own repeated predictions.
Matthew notes the stolen-body story was still circulating when he wrote; by recording it he answers the counter-claim, which itself presupposes an empty tomb.
